questions.question: Can you walk me through your background?
include: Open with Staff-level Product Designer and design leader.
Mention 12 years of experience at Baidu and Alibaba.
Highlight Baidu App's scale (200M DAU) and Duxiaoxiao as an avatar-based AI assistant.
Position the CS Master's as a bridge for design craft, not a career pivot.
End with Vancouver location and a company-tailored closing hook.
avoid: Starting with education before professional positioning.
Listing every project without a clear theme.
Framing yourself as a pure engineer, manager, or visual designer.
Overclaiming company-level results as individual achievements.
Rambling for more than 2 minutes; keep the opening response concise.
Using overly academic or formal language instead of conversational English.
questions.standardAnswer:
Certainly, I'd be happy to walk you through my background.

I’m a Staff Product Designer and design leader with 12 years of experience, including 10 years at Baidu and 2 years at Alibaba. Throughout my career, I've focused on bridging deep design craft with technical fluency and business strategy.

During my time at Baidu, I served as the Design Architect for Baidu App, the company's flagship product serving over 200 million daily active users. I led high-impact initiatives like homepage redesigns and core design infrastructure, helping drive both user growth and monetization.

I also led the 0-to-1 design of Duxiaoxiao, China's first AI assistant built around an interactive 3D avatar. By defining its multimodal experience and scalable production systems, I helped scale the product to tens of millions of users and billions of interactions.

To bridge my design craft with engineering, I recently completed a master's degree in Applied Computer Science. I’m now based in Vancouver and looking for product design opportunities where I can apply my systems thinking, AI experience, and technical fluency to solve complex product problems.

questions.question: How would you describe your current job search?
include: State that you are currently based in Vancouver and looking for product design opportunities.
Keep it concise and recruiter-friendly.
Mention target roles broadly enough to preserve opportunity access.
Include availability if relevant.
avoid: Giving a long career-transition story unless asked.
Sounding uncertain about whether you are a designer or engineer.
Overly narrowing the search to only Staff-level roles.
questions.standardAnswer: I am currently based in Vancouver, Canada, and I am looking for product design opportunities. I am primarily targeting Product Designer, Senior Product Designer, Staff Product Designer, UX/UI Designer, AI Product Designer, Design Systems Designer, and Product Design Lead roles. I am open to remote, hybrid, or onsite work, with a preference for Canada-based opportunities. I am available to interview immediately and can start immediately.

questions.question: What kinds of roles are you targeting?
include: Mention product design as the primary direction.
Include Senior, Staff, AI Product Designer, Design Systems, and Product Design Lead roles.
Explain role fit through experience, not title preference only.
Keep flexibility around level and scope.
avoid: Saying you only want management or only want IC work.
Over-positioning as an engineer because of the computer science master's degree.
questions.standardAnswer: I am targeting product design roles where my experience in complex consumer products, AI interaction, design systems, and cross-functional leadership can be useful. The titles I am looking at include Product Designer, Senior Product Designer, Staff Product Designer, AI Product Designer, Design Systems Designer, UX/UI Designer, and Product Design Lead. I am most interested in teams where design is expected to contribute to product direction, user experience quality, experimentation, and implementation quality, beyond interface delivery.

questions.question: Why are you interested in this role?
include: Connect the company role to your strongest background: platform UX, AI, design systems, product strategy, or business impact.
Mention the product problem, not only the brand.
Show you understand what the role needs.
Use a concise bridge from past experience to future contribution.
avoid: Generic praise such as "your company is famous."
Repeating the job description without a personal point of view.
Over-indexing on learning opportunities instead of contribution.
questions.standardAnswer: I am interested in this role because it seems to need a designer who can work beyond isolated interface execution. My strongest experience is in complex product environments where user needs, business goals, technical constraints, and organizational alignment all matter. At Baidu, I worked on products with hundreds of millions of users, led AI assistant and design infrastructure work, and often had to connect product strategy with measurable outcomes. This role feels like a strong fit because I can bring a systems lens to product design while also staying hands-on in UX, UI, prototyping, and cross-functional execution.

questions.question: Why are you looking for a new opportunity now?
include: Explain the transition as intentional growth, not escape.
Mention leaving Baidu to broaden technical foundation and market perspective.
Connect Canada and the master's degree to long-term product design capability.
Emphasize readiness to return to product design work.
avoid: Criticizing Baidu, China, or previous teams.
Sounding like the career gap was unplanned or unmanaged.
Making the master's degree sound like a pivot away from design.
questions.standardAnswer: After 10 years at Baidu, I wanted to step outside one major product ecosystem and broaden both my technical foundation and product perspective. I have always believed that technology is one of the core materials of digital product design, so I moved to Canada and completed a master's degree in Applied Computer Science. That experience helped me better understand software systems, machine learning, web applications, and Web3D, while also giving me a broader view of the North American product environment. Now I am ready to return to product design work and bring together my design leadership, AI product experience, and technical fluency.

questions.question: Why did you leave Baidu?
include: Frame it as a planned long-term capability upgrade after 10 years.
Mention comfort zone, broader market perspective, and technical depth.
Clarify the visa-processing gap if necessary.
Keep tone professional and positive.
avoid: Saying you left because of dissatisfaction.
Making the move sound disconnected from product design.
questions.standardAnswer: I left Baidu after 10 years because I wanted to step outside my comfort zone and upgrade my long-term capability as a technology product designer. At Baidu, I experienced the full mobile internet cycle and worked on large-scale search, content, AI assistant, and platform design problems. The next step I wanted was to strengthen my technical foundation and broaden my perspective beyond one market. That is why I moved to Canada for a master's degree in Applied Computer Science. The transition was intentional, and it has made me better prepared for product design roles that sit closer to AI, systems, and implementation.

questions.question: Why did you move to Canada?
include: Mention education, broader market perspective, and long-term career growth.
State current Vancouver location clearly.
Connect Canada to North American product context.
Keep immigration/work authorization separate unless asked.
avoid: Making the move sound random or only lifestyle-driven.
Suggesting uncertainty about staying in Canada.
questions.standardAnswer: I moved to Canada to broaden my technical and product perspective after many years working in China's technology industry. I chose a master's program in Applied Computer Science because I wanted to understand technology more deeply as a designer, especially for AI, web applications, and interactive systems. I am now based in Vancouver and looking for product design opportunities where I can apply my previous large-scale consumer product experience in a North American product environment.

questions.question: How does your master's degree in Applied Computer Science relate to your design career?
include: Frame technology as a design material.
Mention stronger collaboration with engineers and AI/technical teams.
Highlight courses: data structures, algorithms, databases, operating systems, software engineering, machine learning, web apps, Web3D.
Clarify you remain a designer.
avoid: Positioning yourself as a software engineer candidate.
Getting too technical for a recruiter screen.
Treating the degree as unrelated to prior design experience.
questions.standardAnswer: I see the master's degree as an extension of my design career, not a pivot away from design. For technology product designers, technology is like a material. If you understand the material better, you can make better product decisions and collaborate more effectively with engineering teams. In the program, I studied computer science fundamentals such as algorithms, databases, operating systems, software engineering, machine learning, web application development, and Web3D. That helps me design more feasible interactions, build stronger prototypes, and communicate with engineers with more precision, especially in AI, 3D, and complex frontend experiences.

questions.question: Are you authorized to work in Canada?
include: State the fact directly.
Mention that the three-year Canadian post-graduation work permit is being processed.
Confirm no employer sponsorship is needed for Canada.
questions.standardAnswer: Yes. My three-year Canadian post-graduation work permit is currently being processed, and I currently have legal authorization to work for Canadian employers. I do not need employer sponsorship to work in Canada.

questions.question: Are you open to remote, hybrid, or onsite work?
include: State openness to all three.
Mention Canada preference.
Show flexibility for the right opportunity.
avoid: Being overly rigid early in the process.
Committing to relocation without knowing the role.
questions.standardAnswer: Yes. I am open to remote, hybrid, and onsite work. My preference is Canada-based opportunities, but for the right role and team, I am also open to discussing other locations or working models.

questions.question: What are your compensation expectations?
include: Defer exact number until scope, level, and total compensation are clear.
Signal market awareness.
Keep the answer collaborative.
If pressed, provide a range only after local market research.
avoid: Anchoring too low out of nervousness.
Giving a rigid number before understanding level.
Talking only about salary and ignoring total compensation.
questions.standardAnswer: I am still calibrating based on level, scope, location, and total compensation structure. My priority is finding the right product design role where the scope matches my experience and where I can have meaningful impact. If we move forward, I would be happy to discuss a market-aligned range based on the level and compensation structure for this role.

questions.question: Can you briefly explain the scale of Baidu App for a North American audience?
include: Translate, do not assume familiarity.
Mention search, content discovery, short video, AI assistants, and services.
Include scale: 700M MAU, 200M DAU.
Use analogy carefully without claiming exact equivalence.
avoid: Saying "it is like Google" and stopping there.
questions.standardAnswer: Baidu App is Baidu's flagship mobile search and information app. For a North American audience, I would describe it as a China-specific super app-style product that combines parts of Google Search, Google Discover, YouTube Shorts, Google Assistant, and service entry points inside one high-frequency mobile app. It had about 700 million monthly active users and 200 million daily active users, so even small homepage or interaction changes needed careful design judgment, experimentation, and rollout.

questions.question: What type of design work are you strongest at?
include: Name 3-4 strongest areas: platform UX, AI product design, design systems, outcome-driven product design.
Mention hands-on craft plus strategic judgment.
Support with Baidu App and Duxiaoxiao.
avoid: Giving a generic list of tools.
Overemphasizing visual craft without product judgment.
questions.standardAnswer: I am strongest in complex product design where the problem is bigger than a single flow or screen. My strongest areas are large-scale platform UX, AI product experience, design systems, and outcome-driven product design. At Baidu App, I worked on homepage redesigns, search and algorithmic-feed experiences, and design infrastructure for a product with hundreds of millions of users. At Duxiaoxiao, I led 0-to-1 AI virtual human assistant design, including multimodal interaction, character systems, AI + search integration, and scalable 3D production workflows. I can move from strategy to execution across product direction, UX architecture, hands-on UI craft, prototyping, and implementation detail, which is useful when a team needs both direction and delivery.

questions.question: Are you more of an IC designer, a design lead, or a people manager?
include: Use hybrid IC/people-manager framing.
Mention hands-on ownership and direct management experience.
Explain you can adapt based on role needs.
avoid: Sounding unwilling to do hands-on work.
Sounding like management is the only acceptable path.
questions.standardAnswer: My background is closest to a hybrid IC and design lead profile. My hybrid background lets me operate as both a senior IC and a design lead. I have 6 years of direct people-management experience and have managed cross-functional design teams, including interaction, visual, and 3D designers. At the same time, I have continued to own high-priority hands-on design work, especially around product architecture, AI interaction frameworks, design systems, and executive-level design direction. I am comfortable as a senior IC, Staff-level designer, or product design lead, as long as the role needs both strong hands-on design judgment and cross-functional influence.

questions.question: What does Staff-level product design mean in your experience?
include: Define Staff scope beyond execution.
Mention product direction, systems, cross-team influence, quality standards, and measurable outcomes.
Use Baidu App as evidence.
avoid: Defining Staff only by years of experience.
Making it sound like Staff means less design craft.
questions.standardAnswer: In my experience, Staff-level product design means working at a level where the design problem often includes product direction, systems, organizational alignment, and measurable outcomes, not only interface execution. On Baidu App, for example, homepage redesign was not just a UI change. It involved market shifts, user habits, content distribution strategy, business goals, engineering feasibility, and stakeholder alignment across many teams. A Staff-level designer needs to clarify the problem, make trade-offs explicit, align people around a direction, and still care deeply about the quality of what ships.

questions.question: What kind of problems do you tend to add the most value to?
include: Complex, ambiguous, cross-functional, high-scale product problems.
AI, platform, design systems, growth/monetization trade-offs.
Problems where design must connect users, business, and technology.
avoid: Choosing problems too narrow for your background.
questions.standardAnswer: I add the most value to complex product problems where the team needs to connect user needs, business goals, technical constraints, and long-term experience quality. Examples include platform-scale homepage redesigns, AI assistant interaction models, design systems, monetization surfaces, and product infrastructure used by many teams. I am especially useful when the problem is ambiguous at the beginning and needs to be turned into a clear experience framework, phased delivery plan, and measurable success criteria.

questions.question: How would you summarize your Baidu App experience?
include: Explain Baidu App for a North American audience.
Mention evolution from search tool to information and services platform.
Highlight homepage, algorithmic feed, AI + search, design infrastructure.
Include impact and scale.
questions.standardAnswer: Baidu App was my largest platform UX experience. It is Baidu's flagship mobile search and information app, with about 700 million monthly active users and 200 million daily active users. I worked on it across multiple stages as it evolved from a search-focused mobile app into a broader platform for search, content discovery, short video, local services, and AI assistants. My work included homepage redesigns, the shift toward a search-and-algorithmic-feed architecture, design infrastructure such as dark mode and motion systems, and AI + search exploration. That experience shaped how I think about platform-scale design, experimentation, stakeholder alignment, and business impact.

questions.question: How would you summarize your Duxiaoxiao experience?
include: Explain it as China's first interactive AI virtual human assistant.
Clarify it was not just a chatbot or visual character.
Mention multimodal interaction, AI + search, 3D asset systems, monetization, awards.
Show mature reflection on what worked and what did not.
avoid: Presenting it as an LLM product if the timing predates mainstream LLMs.
Overstating it as a continuing product after further investment ended in early 2023.
questions.standardAnswer: Duxiaoxiao was China's first interactive AI virtual human assistant, an avatar-style AI experience before the mainstream LLM era. It was not just a chatbot or a 3D character; it was an attempt to bring AI capabilities into real user flows through a more humanized, multimodal interaction model. I led the design team through 0-to-1 experience definition, AI + search integration inside Baidu App, multimodal interaction across voice, facial expressions, motion, lip sync, and interface controls, and the creation of character and 3D production systems for content and commercial scenarios. The work supported billions of conversations, RMB 100M-level monetization revenue, 3 patents granted in China, and 6 international design awards. Further investment ended in early 2023 as the team shifted toward LLM + search, and that experience made my AI product judgment more grounded: AI needs to improve a real user path, not only feel expressive or novel.

questions.question: What differentiates you from other senior product designers?
include: Combine product judgment, systems thinking, AI experience, technical fluency, business impact, and leadership.
Use evidence, not self-praise.
Position China-scale experience as transferable.
avoid: Saying you are simply more experienced.
questions.standardAnswer: My differentiation is the combination of large-scale product design, AI product experience, technical fluency, business awareness, and design leadership. I have designed for consumer products with hundreds of millions of users, led 0-to-1 AI assistant experiences, built platform-level design infrastructure, and connected design decisions to measurable business impact. I also have enough technical background to collaborate closely with engineering and build high-fidelity prototypes when static design is not enough. So my value is not limited to UX/UI execution; I can help shape product direction, create systems, align teams, and improve the quality of what actually ships.

questions.question: How do you balance product strategy and hands-on design execution?
include: Explain movement between altitude levels.
Use frameworks, prototypes, and experiments to connect strategy to execution.
Mention staying hands-on while leading.
avoid: Treating strategy and execution as separate tracks.
Suggesting you only delegate craft work.
questions.standardAnswer: I try to connect strategy and execution through concrete experience frameworks. At the strategy level, I clarify the user problem, business goal, market context, and technical constraints. Then I translate constraints into design decisions across information architecture, interaction models, prototypes, design standards, and success metrics. In the Baidu App homepage redesign, the strategy was to move the product toward stronger content discovery while preserving search. The hands-on work included homepage hierarchy, feed prominence, search entry behavior, experiment variants, and rollout decisions. I think good senior design work needs both levels because strategy only matters if it can survive execution.

questions.question: Tell me about a time when you shaped product direction, not just interface execution.
include: Use Baidu App homepage redesign.
Explain strategic judgment: algorithmic feed becoming central in mobile internet.
Mention stakeholder alignment, experiments, and impact.
Clarify your design role.
avoid: Claiming you alone decided company strategy.
questions.standardAnswer: One strong example is the Baidu App homepage redesign. At the time, Baidu App still carried a strong search-first mental model, but the mobile internet market was shifting toward content discovery and algorithmic feeds. I strongly believed the homepage needed to move toward a search-and-algorithmic-feed architecture, where search remained core but the feed had a more prominent role in daily engagement. My role was to turn that product judgment into a design direction, align product and business stakeholders, explore different homepage hierarchies, and validate the direction through limited-traffic experiments and reversal experiments. The 2019-2020 redesign, especially the 2020 homepage upgrade, improved engagement metrics, and the data science team estimated roughly RMB 100 million in annualized revenue growth. That was a case where design helped shape the product model, not just the interface.

questions.question: How do you approach ambiguous or poorly defined product problems?
include: Start by clarifying users, business goals, constraints, and unknowns.
Break ambiguity into workstreams.
Use prototypes and evidence to align teams.
Mention Duxiaoxiao or younger-user strategy.
avoid: Waiting passively for PMs to define everything.
questions.standardAnswer: I usually start by separating what we know, what we assume, and what we need to learn. Then I clarify the user value, business goal, technical constraints, stakeholders, and success criteria. For Duxiaoxiao, the initial direction of an AI virtual human assistant was very broad, so I broke it into workstreams: interaction framework, character behavior, multimodal expression, search integration, 3D asset production, and commercial use cases. Prototypes were especially important because static screens could not explain AI, motion, 3D, and conversation clearly. My goal in ambiguous problems is to turn uncertainty into a clear path that teams can discuss, test, and execute.

questions.question: How do you work when the business goal and user experience are in tension?
include: Acknowledge tension as normal.
Make trade-offs explicit.
Seek shared value and guardrails.
Use homepage monetization infrastructure as evidence.
avoid: Saying user experience always wins without context.
questions.standardAnswer: I treat that tension as part of the design problem. The first step is to make the trade-offs explicit: what business outcome are we optimizing for, what user cost might we create, and what risk are we accepting? In Baidu App, the homepage was both a core user entry point and a commercial surface. For in-product campaigns and monetization, I helped create placement rules, mutual-exclusion rules, templates, and a review process so the homepage could support business needs without overwhelming the core experience. I try to find shared value: a solution that supports the business while protecting clarity, trust, and long-term user behavior.

questions.question: What kinds of design systems or platform work have you led?
include: Mention dark mode, motion system, campaign/monetization infrastructure, Duxiaoxiao character asset system.
Explain adoption and cross-team use.
Frame systems as standards, tooling, governance, and implementation quality.
avoid: Reducing design systems to UI components only.
Listing systems without explaining why they mattered.
questions.standardAnswer: I have led several platform and design infrastructure efforts. For Baidu's mobile product ecosystem, I led dark mode standards covering visual hierarchy, readability, low-light usage, hardware constraints, and engineering rollout across many teams. For Baidu App, I led motion system work, including motion principles, parameter standards, and implementation mechanisms using Lottie and internal video-based motion capabilities. I also helped build homepage campaign and monetization infrastructure with reusable rules, templates, and review processes. For Duxiaoxiao, I led the character and brand asset system and 3D production workflows. I see design systems as more than components; they are reusable decisions, tooling, governance, and collaboration mechanisms.

questions.question: What kind of AI product design experience do you have?
include: Duxiaoxiao as main AI product.
AI + search and LLM + search exploration.
Trust, multimodal interaction, conversation/search intent switching.
Clarify not ML research.
avoid: Talking about AI only in current buzzwords.
questions.standardAnswer: My AI product design experience comes mainly from Duxiaoxiao and AI + search exploration at Baidu. Duxiaoxiao was China's first interactive AI virtual human assistant, and I led the team in defining the interaction model, multimodal expression, character behavior, search integration, and production workflows. We had to decide how users would invoke the assistant, how the system would distinguish conversational intent from search intent, how to switch between AI conversation and search results, and how voice, facial expression, motion, and UI states could make the AI feel understandable. In 2023, my team also worked on LLM + search design exploration. My focus is not algorithm research; it is how AI capabilities enter real user flows in a trustworthy, useful, and scalable way.

questions.question: Walk us through a project you are proud of.
include: Choose one flagship case and state why it matters.
Use a clear narrative: context, problem, role, process, trade-offs, outcome, learning.
For general roles, lead with Baidu App homepage redesign.
avoid: Starting with visuals before product context.
Taking more than 8-10 minutes without checkpoints.
questions.standardAnswer: One project I am proud of is the Baidu App homepage redesign, especially the 2019-2020 work that led to the 2020 homepage upgrade. Baidu App was a very high-scale product with about 200 million daily active users, and the homepage was the most important entry point for search, content discovery, user engagement, and monetization. The core challenge was that the market had shifted toward algorithmic feeds and time-spent competition, while Baidu App still carried a strong search-first homepage structure. My role as design architect was to help move the homepage toward a search-and-algorithmic-feed model without breaking the existing search mental model. We explored multiple hierarchy options, aligned stakeholders, validated through A/B and reversal experiments, and gradually moved the product toward a cleaner and more engagement-oriented structure. The redesign improved key engagement metrics, and the data science team estimated roughly RMB 100 million in annualized revenue growth.

questions.question: What was the problem you were solving?
include: State the user, business, and product-architecture problem.
Explain market shift from active search to content discovery.
Mention preserving search clarity while increasing feed prominence.
avoid: Saying the problem was just "the homepage looked old."
questions.standardAnswer: The problem was not simply visual redesign. Baidu App needed to evolve its homepage product model. Users still needed fast access to search, but mobile behavior had shifted toward continuous content discovery through algorithmic feeds. From the business side, engagement, time spent, and downstream conversion had become increasingly important. So the design problem was how to give the algorithmic feed a more central role while preserving the clarity and trust of search, and how to do that safely on a product with hundreds of millions of users.

questions.question: What was your specific role in the project?
include: State design architect/project design lead.
Clarify ownership: design strategy, experience direction, exploration, alignment, experiment iteration.
Mention collaboration with PM, data, engineering, and other business teams.
avoid: Claiming sole ownership of every decision.
questions.standardAnswer: I was the project design lead and design architect. My role included shaping the experience strategy, defining homepage hierarchy directions, guiding design exploration, aligning cross-functional stakeholders, and working with product, engineering, and data teams through experiment design and iteration. I also managed designers on the project, but I stayed hands-on in the most important product and interaction decisions. Because the homepage affected many business teams, a major part of my role was making the design rationale clear enough for stakeholders to understand what we were optimizing for and what risks we were managing.

questions.question: Who were the key stakeholders and collaborators?
include: Mention PMs, engineers, data scientists/analysts, business owners, design team, leadership.
Explain what each group contributed.
Show how you aligned them.
avoid: Making collaboration sound like one-way persuasion.
questions.standardAnswer: The key collaborators included product managers responsible for homepage and growth, engineering teams responsible for implementation and experiment rollout, data scientists who helped evaluate metrics and estimate business impact, business stakeholders affected by homepage traffic, and design leadership. My design team explored and refined the experience direction. I used prototypes, clear comparison frameworks, experiment results, and design rationale to keep the discussion grounded, because different teams cared about different outcomes: search efficiency, feed engagement, revenue, implementation risk, and user perception.

questions.question: What constraints did you have to work within?
include: Scale risk, existing user mental model, business pressure, technical feasibility, stakeholder complexity.
Mention phased rollout and experiments.
questions.standardAnswer: The biggest constraint was scale. With a product at that level of daily usage, even a small homepage change could affect user behavior, business revenue, and many downstream teams. We also had to respect the existing user mental model around search. If we pushed the feed too aggressively, we could damage clarity and trust. There were also business constraints because many teams depended on homepage traffic, and technical constraints around implementation and experiment rollout. That is why we used phased exploration, limited-traffic experiments, and reversal experiments rather than making one large unvalidated change.

questions.question: What were the user needs behind the project?
include: Fast search access, relevant content discovery, clean homepage, low cognitive load.
Mention changing mobile behavior.
Balance active intent and passive discovery.
avoid: Describing users as only traffic or engagement.
Assuming everyone wanted more feed content.
questions.standardAnswer: The core user needs were fast access to search, easy discovery of relevant content, and a homepage that did not feel noisy or confusing. Baidu App users came with both active intent and passive discovery behavior. Sometimes they wanted to search for something directly; sometimes they were open to reading news, watching short videos, or discovering content based on their interests. The design needed to support both modes without making search feel buried or making the homepage feel overly commercial.

questions.question: What were the business goals behind the project?
include: DAU, time spent, search/feed engagement, downstream conversion, monetization.
Connect goals to product strategy.
Keep business goals balanced with user experience.
avoid: Pretending business goals did not matter.
questions.standardAnswer: The business goals were to improve Baidu App's overall engagement and strengthen the homepage as a product growth engine. Key metrics included daily active users, time spent, conversion from homepage visits into search, and engagement with the algorithmic feed. There was also downstream business value because stronger engagement and better attention routing could support monetization. My focus was to connect those goals with a user experience that still felt clear, useful, and trustworthy.

questions.question: How did you define success?
include: Define both quantitative and qualitative success.
Mention DAU, time spent, search/feed conversion, user satisfaction, clarity.
Include implementation and stakeholder adoption where relevant.
questions.standardAnswer: We defined success through a combination of engagement, conversion, user perception, and rollout quality. Quantitatively, we looked at DAU, time spent, homepage-to-search conversion, homepage-to-feed engagement, and business impact estimates. Qualitatively, we cared about whether users still understood the homepage, whether the search entry remained clear, and whether the experience felt cleaner rather than more cluttered. For a platform homepage, success also meant the design could be adopted by multiple teams without creating long-term maintenance problems.

questions.question: What data or research did you use?
include: Mention A/B testing, limited-traffic experiments, reversal experiments, behavioral metrics, user research.
Explain data-informed, not data-blind.
Include market and competitive judgment.
avoid: Saying "we just used data" without specifics.
Treating qualitative research as less important.
questions.standardAnswer: We used multiple forms of evidence. Quantitatively, we used A/B testing, limited-traffic experiments, reversal experiments, and behavioral metrics such as DAU, time spent, and conversion from homepage visits into search and feed engagement. We also used user research and feedback to understand whether the experience felt cleaner, whether search was still easy to find, and whether the feed prominence felt acceptable. Beyond direct research, we looked at market trends and competitive behavior because algorithmic feeds had become a validated model for mobile content discovery. My approach was data-informed: metrics mattered, but they needed to be interpreted with product judgment and user understanding.

questions.question: What were the main design alternatives you considered?
include: Compare search-first, balanced intermediate, feed-led structures.
Mention hierarchy, search entry, content density, vertical entries.
Explain decision criteria.
avoid: Saying there was only one option.
Leaving out why rejected options failed.
questions.standardAnswer: We considered several homepage structures. One option was to keep the search-first structure with only a secondary feed, which protected the existing mental model but limited content discovery. Another was a more balanced structure that reduced some traditional vertical entries and made the feed more visible. The most progressive option made the algorithmic feed the primary homepage experience while keeping search clear and accessible. We compared these options based on user clarity, engagement potential, business impact, implementation risk, and how much change users could accept at each stage.

questions.question: What trade-offs did you make?
include: Feed prominence vs search clarity.
Engagement vs visual simplicity.
Business needs vs user trust.
Speed vs evidence.
avoid: Presenting every trade-off as perfectly solved.
questions.standardAnswer: The main trade-off was between increasing feed prominence and protecting search clarity. If the homepage stayed too search-heavy, we would miss the opportunity to improve content discovery and engagement. If we moved too aggressively toward the feed, users might feel that search was less central or that the product had become noisy. We also had to balance business needs with user trust, because the homepage carried monetization and campaign requirements. We accepted a phased approach because it was slower, but it gave us better evidence and reduced the risk of disrupting a very large user base.

questions.question: How did the design evolve over time?
include: Explain multi-year evolution from search + verticals to search + algorithmic feed.
Mention 2017, 2018, 2019/2020 stages if useful.
Show strategic persistence and evidence-based iteration.
avoid: Treating the final design as a one-time inspiration.
Leaving out why gradual change mattered.
questions.standardAnswer: The design evolved gradually over several generations. Earlier homepage versions had search, vertical service entry points, and contextual cards. Then we introduced and increased the role of the algorithmic feed, first as an exploratory layer and later as a more central experience. By the 2019/2020 redesign, the homepage moved much closer to a search-and-algorithmic-feed model. That gradual evolution was important because users had a strong search mental model, and the organization had many stakeholders depending on homepage structure. Each stage helped us validate direction and reduce risk before moving further.

questions.question: How did you validate the design?
include: A/B tests, limited-traffic experiments, reversal experiments, user research, metric monitoring.
Mention guardrails and iteration.
avoid: Saying validation was only stakeholder approval.
questions.standardAnswer: We validated the design through a combination of user research, high-fidelity prototypes, limited-traffic experiments, A/B testing, and reversal experiments. The experiments helped us compare the redesigned homepage against the existing version and observe changes in DAU, time spent, search conversion, and feed engagement. We also monitored user perception and experience quality so we were not optimizing only for short-term engagement. For a platform at that scale, validation needed to happen in production with real users, but in a controlled and staged way.

questions.question: What did the treatment group and control group see?
include: Explain simply.
Treatment saw redesigned homepage; control saw existing version.
Mention variable isolation where possible.
avoid: Getting too technical unless interviewer asks.
Claiming perfect isolation if not true.
questions.standardAnswer: The control group continued to see the existing homepage experience. The treatment group saw the redesigned homepage with adjusted hierarchy, stronger algorithmic-feed prominence, and a cleaner structure around search and content discovery. We tried to keep other major variables stable so the team could better understand whether changes in behavior were related to the homepage design itself. In later reversal experiments, we swapped configurations to reduce the risk that the result came from audience differences rather than the design change.

questions.question: How did you know the results were caused by the design change?
include: Mention controlled experiments, traffic splitting, reversal experiments, consistent metric movement.
Acknowledge limits of confidence.
Explain triangulation.
questions.standardAnswer: We could not claim perfect certainty, but we reduced risk through experiment design. We used traffic-split experiments so the treatment and control groups could be compared during the same period. We also used reversal experiments, where the configurations were swapped, to check whether the metric movement followed the design rather than a particular user segment. We looked for consistent movement across DAU, time spent, and search/feed engagement, and we interpreted the data alongside user feedback and product context. For the revenue estimate, the data science team used the best available method to isolate the homepage redesign, but I would still describe it as an estimate rather than an absolute fact.

questions.question: What metrics moved after launch?
include: Mention DAU, time spent, homepage-to-search/feed conversion, engagement.
Mention estimated RMB 100M annualized revenue growth with caveat.
Include qualitative results if available.
questions.standardAnswer: The redesign improved key product metrics including Baidu App DAU, time spent, and conversion from homepage visits into search and algorithmic-feed engagement. Based on the experiment results, the data science team estimated that the 2020 homepage upgrade generated roughly several hundred thousand RMB in incremental revenue per day, or about RMB 100 million in annualized revenue growth. I would present that as an estimate with some statistical-confidence limitations, because the data systems could not perfectly trace homepage-level changes to final revenue. User research also indicated that the experience felt cleaner and more orderly.

questions.question: What would you do differently now?
include: Show reflection without undermining the project.
Mention deeper qualitative research, guardrails, personalization, long-term retention, accessibility.
For current North American context, mention clearer documentation and communication.
questions.standardAnswer: I would still support the main direction, but I would invest even more in understanding different user segments and long-term behavior. At that time, we were very focused on validating homepage hierarchy and engagement impact, which made sense for the business context. Now I would add more guardrail metrics around content quality, perceived trust, and long-term retention, not just immediate engagement. I would also document the decision framework more explicitly so future teams could understand why each trade-off was made, not only what design shipped.

questions.question: What was the hardest design decision in this project?
include: Choose feed prominence vs search identity.
Explain why it was difficult.
Describe how evidence guided the decision.
avoid: Making the answer sound purely subjective.
questions.standardAnswer: The hardest decision was how far to move the homepage away from its traditional search-first identity. Baidu App had a strong user mental model around search, and that was also the product's core advantage. At the same time, the market was clearly moving toward algorithmic content discovery, and the homepage needed to support engagement in a more competitive environment. We had to decide how much prominence the feed should receive without making search feel secondary. The decision was difficult because it affected user trust, business metrics, and many internal teams, so we relied on staged experiments and user feedback rather than one big subjective decision.

questions.question: What part of the project was most controversial?
include: Explain stakeholder tension around homepage real estate and traffic.
Mention search-first identity vs feed-led growth.
Show conflict management.
questions.standardAnswer: The most controversial part was homepage priority. Different teams depended on the homepage for search traffic, feed engagement, vertical service entry points, campaigns, and monetization. Moving toward a stronger algorithmic feed meant some existing entry points became less prominent, which naturally created concern. I tried to handle that by making the design rationale explicit, comparing alternatives, using data from experiments, and separating short-term traffic loss concerns from long-term product direction. The goal was not to make every team equally happy, but to help the organization make a better product-level decision.

questions.question: How did you communicate the design direction to leadership?
include: Use business language plus user experience rationale.
Show alternatives, trade-offs, evidence, and recommendation.
Keep the narrative clear.
questions.standardAnswer: For leadership communication, I focused on the product decision, not only the interface. I explained the market shift, the user behavior change, the business opportunity, and the risk of staying with the old homepage model. Then I showed the design alternatives, the trade-offs behind each one, experiment results, and my recommendation. I also made the risks clear: how users might react, what metrics we needed to watch, and why a phased rollout was safer. That helped leadership evaluate the design as a product strategy decision rather than a visual preference.

questions.question: How did you work with engineering during implementation?
include: Mention early feasibility, prototypes, experiment variants, implementation fidelity, rollout.
Show respect for engineering constraints.
Use technical fluency.
questions.standardAnswer: I worked with engineering early, especially because homepage changes affected performance, experiment setup, release timing, and multiple product modules. I used prototypes and detailed interaction specs to clarify behavior, but I also discussed feasibility and implementation risk with engineers before the design became too fixed. During rollout, we needed to support experiment variants, monitor behavior, and make sure implementation fidelity was high. My technical fluency helped because I could discuss frontend constraints and interaction details more precisely instead of only handing off static screens.

questions.question: How did this project change the product or business?
include: Explain product architecture shift and lasting model.
Mention engagement and estimated revenue impact.
Include broader organizational impact.
avoid: Overclaiming that design alone changed the business.
questions.standardAnswer: The project helped Baidu App move toward the search-and-algorithmic-feed architecture that became its long-term homepage model. It improved engagement metrics such as DAU, time spent, and conversion into search and feed usage, and the data science team estimated roughly RMB 100 million in annualized revenue growth from the 2020 homepage upgrade. Beyond the numbers, it changed how the organization thought about the homepage: not just as a search entry point, but as a platform surface that routes user attention across search, content discovery, and business needs. That made the design impact both experiential and strategic.

questions.question: Walk us through Duxiaoxiao as a portfolio case.
include: Explain Duxiaoxiao as China's first interactive AI virtual human assistant.
Clarify the product context before mainstream LLMs.
State your role as Design Lead and Design Architect.
Cover problem, process, trade-offs, outcomes, and learning.
avoid: Making it sound like only a 3D character project.
Presenting it as an LLM-native product.
Overclaiming company-level results as individual-only outcomes.
questions.standardAnswer:
Duxiaoxiao was China's first interactive AI virtual human assistant, an avatar-style AI experience before the mainstream LLM era. The product explored whether AI capabilities could become more understandable and approachable through multimodal interaction, including voice, facial expression, motion, lip sync, character behavior, and interface controls.

My role was Design Lead and Design Architect. I led the team through 0-to-1 experience definition, the mobile interaction framework, AI + search integration inside Baidu App, and the character and 3D production systems used for content and commercial scenarios.

The biggest challenge was that this was not a mature product category. We had to define how a virtual human should enter a search flow, when it should speak, how expressive it should be, and how users could switch between conversation and traditional search results. We used prototypes, production-scale validation, and continuous iteration to turn a broad AI vision into a real product experience.

The work reached tens of millions of users inside Baidu App, accumulated billions of conversations, supported RMB 100M-level monetization revenue, and won 6 international design awards. More importantly, it shaped how I think about AI product design: emotional expression can make AI more approachable, but durable value still depends on usefulness, trust, control, and whether the AI improves a real user path.

questions.question: What was your specific contribution to Duxiaoxiao?
include: Separate personal design contribution from AI algorithm ownership.
Mention experience framework, AI + search integration, multimodal interaction, and 3D production workflow.
Show leadership and hands-on design judgment.
Clarify that you were not the ML algorithm owner.
avoid: Reducing the contribution to visual or character design.
questions.standardAnswer:
My contribution was mainly in four areas. First, I defined the core experience framework for an AI virtual human assistant, including the spatial layer, character layer, content container, and control layer. Second, I led the AI + search integration inside Baidu App, where users could move between conversational interaction and traditional search results depending on intent. Third, I guided the multimodal interaction system, including voice, facial expression, motion, lip sync, and UI feedback. Fourth, I led the character asset and 3D production workflow so the team could support product, brand, content, and commercial use cases at scale.

I was not the algorithm owner, and I would not describe myself as an ML engineer. My role was to translate AI capabilities into product experiences that users could understand, trust, and use.

questions.question: What was the hardest design decision in Duxiaoxiao?
include: Choose a meaningful product trade-off.
Explain human-like expression versus product efficiency.
Mention search users' need for efficiency and answer quality.
Show how this changed your AI product judgment.
avoid: Ignoring search efficiency and user expectations.
questions.standardAnswer:
The hardest decision was balancing human-like expression with product efficiency. A virtual human can make AI feel warmer and more approachable, but if the expression slows down the user or creates unrealistic expectations, it can hurt the experience.

For search users, efficiency and answer quality mattered more than performance or personality. So we had to decide when Duxiaoxiao should be expressive, when the interface should stay simple, and when the experience should hand off to traditional search results. That trade-off helped me understand that AI personality should serve the user goal, not become the goal itself.

questions.question: How do we know what impact was yours versus the team's or company's?
include: Acknowledge that large-scale outcomes are team and company outcomes.
Clarify your personal ownership and decision influence.
Use Baidu App and Duxiaoxiao as examples.
Avoid claiming sole credit for revenue, users, or awards.
avoid: Taking sole credit for company-level impact.
Being so modest that your role becomes unclear.
Using vague language like "I helped" without specifics.
questions.standardAnswer:
That is an important distinction. I would not claim that company-level outcomes were created by me alone. Products like Baidu App and Duxiaoxiao involved product, engineering, data, AI, business, marketing, and leadership teams.

My personal impact was in the design judgment and execution path: defining the experience direction, creating frameworks, aligning stakeholders, guiding design exploration, using prototypes to reduce ambiguity, and helping teams make trade-offs that could actually ship. For Baidu App homepage, the data science team estimated the business impact, but my contribution was shaping the homepage direction, pushing the search-and-algorithmic-feed architecture, and leading design through experiments and rollout. For Duxiaoxiao, my contribution was turning an abstract AI virtual human concept into product frameworks, interaction models, and scalable production workflows.

So I frame the results as team or company outcomes, and I explain clearly which parts I personally led.

questions.question: What did you personally own on the Baidu App homepage redesign?
include: State design direction and experience architecture ownership.
Mention search-and-algorithmic-feed judgment.
Explain cross-functional alignment and experiment validation.
Separate personal ownership from team-level business results.
avoid: Claiming you alone owned the whole product strategy.
Leaving out PM, engineering, data, and business collaboration.
questions.standardAnswer:
I personally owned the design direction and experience architecture. The homepage was a high-stakes surface with search, feed, monetization, vertical services, and many stakeholder interests competing for space. My role was to clarify the long-term direction: search should remain core, but the algorithmic feed needed stronger prominence as mobile behavior shifted toward content discovery.

I led design exploration across hierarchy, search entry behavior, feed prominence, and phased rollout. I also worked closely with PM, engineering, and data teams to validate the direction through limited-traffic experiments and reversal experiments. The final business result was a team outcome, but my ownership was the design strategy, product framing, interaction direction, and cross-functional alignment behind the redesign.

questions.question: How would you approach redesigning this product flow?
include: Clarify goal, user, context, current friction, constraints, success metrics.
Map the current flow before proposing solutions.
Prioritize and test.
avoid: Designing for yourself instead of target users.
questions.standardAnswer: I would start by clarifying the goal of the flow, the primary user segment, the business objective, and any technical or organizational constraints. Then I would map the current journey and identify where users lose clarity, confidence, or motivation. After that, I would prioritize based on impact, starting with the biggest user friction and the clearest business opportunity. Then I would sketch a few alternative directions and define how we would test them. My goal would be to improve the flow in a way that is easier for users, measurable for the business, and realistic for the team to ship.

questions.question: What problem would you solve first?
include: Prioritize based on user impact, business impact, evidence, and feasibility.
Identify the bottleneck, not the most visible flaw.
Explain your reasoning.
avoid: Picking a surface UI issue without analysis.
questions.standardAnswer: I would solve the problem that creates the biggest user drop-off or misunderstanding while also affecting the product goal. I usually look for the bottleneck: where users hesitate, make errors, abandon the flow, or fail to understand value. If the data shows a clear drop-off, I would start there. If data is not available, I would form a hypothesis based on the journey, then validate it quickly through usability testing or a lightweight experiment. I would not start with visual polish unless the visual issue is directly causing comprehension or trust problems.

questions.question: What assumptions would you want to validate?
include: User intent, comprehension, trust, motivation, information needs, technical feasibility.
Include business assumptions and metric assumptions.
Mention fastest validation method.
questions.standardAnswer: I would validate several assumptions. First, what is the user's real intent when they enter the flow? Second, do they understand the value proposition and the next step? Third, what information do they need before they feel comfortable continuing? Fourth, where are we assuming trust that users may not actually have? I would also validate business assumptions, such as whether the proposed change is likely to improve conversion or retention, and technical assumptions around what can be shipped. Depending on risk, I would use usability testing, prototype testing, analytics review, or a small experiment to validate ideas with real users before scaling the solution.

questions.question: Where do you see the biggest friction?
include: Diagnose based on clarity, effort, trust, decision points, feedback, and information hierarchy.
Tie friction to user behavior.
Offer evidence you would seek.
questions.standardAnswer: I look for friction in a few places: whether users understand what the product is asking them to do, whether the next step is visually and conceptually clear, whether the information hierarchy supports decision-making, and whether users receive feedback after taking action. I also look for trust friction, especially if the product asks for personal information, payment, or reliance on AI output. The biggest friction is usually the place where user uncertainty and product importance overlap. I would confirm that through analytics, session observation, or usability testing.

questions.question: How would you simplify this experience?
include: Reduce decision load, clarify IA, remove redundant steps, prioritize key information.
Preserve necessary complexity.
Mention progressive disclosure.
avoid: Equating simplicity with fewer features only.
Removing business-critical steps without understanding why they exist.
questions.standardAnswer: I would simplify the experience by first identifying what decision the user needs to make at each step. Then I would remove or defer information that does not support that decision. I would also look for repeated choices, unclear labels, unnecessary navigation, and places where the system could provide smarter defaults. But I would not remove complexity blindly. In many products, some complexity is necessary for trust or compliance, so the goal is to make complexity understandable through better hierarchy, grouping, progressive disclosure, and clearer feedback.

questions.question: What information should be more prominent?
include: Prioritize information tied to user decision-making.
Consider context, timing, and intent.
Mention visual hierarchy and scanning.
avoid: Prioritizing business messages over user needs without justification.
Treating prominence as only font size.
questions.standardAnswer: The most prominent information should be whatever helps the user make the next important decision. That usually includes the value of the action, the current state, the primary next step, and any trust or risk information needed to continue. I would use hierarchy, grouping, spacing, contrast, and interaction states to make that information easier to scan. A common mistake is making too many elements compete for attention. Good hierarchy should make the primary path obvious while still keeping secondary information available.

questions.question: What metrics would you use to evaluate your solution?
include: Use primary metric plus guardrail metrics.
Mention task completion, conversion, retention, time, errors, satisfaction, trust.
Tailor to the product.
questions.standardAnswer: I would define one or two primary metrics based on the product goal, such as task completion, conversion, activation, engagement, or retention. Then I would add guardrail metrics to make sure the solution does not create harm, such as error rate, support tickets, time to complete, user satisfaction, trust perception, or long-term retention. For high-scale products, I would also look at segment differences because an improvement for one user group may not help another. My goal is to connect metrics to the actual user behavior we want to improve.

questions.question: How would you test this design before launch?
include: Prototype testing, usability testing, internal review, technical feasibility, A/B testing if possible.
Match validation depth to risk.
Mention staged rollout.
avoid: Launching directly without validation for high-risk changes.
Treating stakeholder approval as user validation.
questions.standardAnswer: I would choose the testing method based on risk. For an early concept, I would use a prototype to test comprehension, task flow, and user expectations. If the design has complex interaction, I would create a high-fidelity prototype because static screens may not reveal the real experience. Before launch, I would review technical feasibility with engineering and define success metrics with product and data partners. For a high-traffic or business-critical flow, I would prefer a staged rollout or A/B test so we can measure impact and catch issues before full release.

questions.question: How would your solution change if engineering capacity were limited?
include: Prioritize high-impact, low-complexity changes.
Separate ideal direction from MVP.
Protect core user value.
Collaborate with engineering early.
avoid: Saying design should ignore engineering constraints.
questions.standardAnswer: If engineering capacity were limited, I would separate the ideal experience from the smallest useful version. I would work with engineering to understand which parts are expensive and which changes are relatively easy. Then I would prioritize improvements that reduce the biggest user friction with the least implementation risk, such as copy, hierarchy, defaults, layout, or reusing existing components. I would still document the longer-term direction, but the first release should protect the core user value and create a path for future iteration.

questions.question: How would you improve trust in this AI feature?
include: Explain AI capability boundaries, confidence, source/citation, state feedback, user control, privacy.
Mention when AI is answering vs searching vs uncertain.
Use Duxiaoxiao experience if appropriate.
avoid: Treating trust as only visual friendliness.
questions.standardAnswer: I would improve trust by making the AI's role, capability, and limitations clear in the interaction. Users need to understand what the AI is doing, whether it is answering, searching, generating, or asking for clarification. I try to build trust through interaction design, not only through copy or policy language. I would provide clear states, confidence signals when appropriate, sources or evidence for factual answers, easy ways to correct or refine the output, and privacy clarity when personal data is involved. From my Duxiaoxiao work, I learned that emotionally expressive interaction can make AI feel more approachable, but trust still depends on usefulness, transparency, control, and consistent behavior.

questions.question: How do you work with product managers?
include: Partner on problem framing, priorities, metrics, trade-offs.
Bring design judgment early.
Use evidence and prototypes.
avoid: Saying design only starts after requirements.
questions.standardAnswer: I work with product managers as partners in problem framing and prioritization. I like to understand the business goal, user problem, constraints, and success metrics early, then contribute design judgment before the solution is fixed. In Baidu App homepage work, for example, I worked closely with PMs to connect market trends, user behavior, homepage architecture, and experiment results. I often use prototypes, comparison frameworks, and data to make trade-offs easier to discuss. Good PM-design collaboration should create a clearer product decision, not just a better-looking interface.

questions.question: How do you work with engineers?
include: Early feasibility discussion, implementation mechanisms, prototyping, fidelity, trade-offs.
Mention technical fluency and respect for constraints.
avoid: Treating engineers as a handoff team.
Going too deep into coding identity.
questions.standardAnswer: I try to involve engineers early, especially when the design includes complex interaction, motion, AI, 3D, or platform constraints. My technical background helps me discuss feasibility, implementation mechanisms, and trade-offs more precisely. For example, in Baidu App's dark mode rollout, we worked with engineering across multiple technology stacks and developed tool-based workflows to improve efficiency. In motion and AI projects, I often used high-fidelity prototypes to reduce ambiguity before engineering investment became too high. I see engineering collaboration as a way to improve both feasibility and final experience quality.

questions.question: How do you work with data scientists or analysts?
include: Define metrics, experiment design, interpretation, causality limits.
Combine data with user insight.
Mention Baidu App experiments.
avoid: Treating data as proof without interpretation.
questions.standardAnswer: I work with data partners early to define what success should mean and how we can measure it responsibly. On Baidu App homepage redesigns, data scientists helped us evaluate DAU, time spent, conversion into search and feed engagement, and estimated business impact. We also used traffic-split experiments and reversal experiments to reduce the risk of misreading results. I do not treat data as a replacement for product judgment; I treat it as evidence that needs to be interpreted with user behavior, research feedback, market context, and experiment limitations.

questions.question: Tell me about a time you disagreed with a PM.
include: Use homepage or monetization example.
Frame disagreement around product trade-offs.
Explain how evidence resolved it.
questions.standardAnswer: One recurring disagreement in Baidu App homepage work was how much homepage space should be given to business or growth needs versus the core user experience. Product partners often had strong goals around engagement or monetization, while I was concerned about clarity, trust, and long-term experience quality. I did not treat it as design versus product. I tried to reframe the discussion around trade-offs: what metric we were trying to move, what user cost we might create, and what guardrails we needed. In the homepage campaign infrastructure work, that led to clearer placement rules, mutual-exclusion rules, and a review process so business goals could be supported without overwhelming the homepage.

questions.question: Tell me about a time engineering pushed back on your design.
include: Use dark mode, motion system, or Duxiaoxiao as example.
Show listening, feasibility adjustment, and quality protection.
Mention prototyping or implementation mechanism.
avoid: Saying engineering pushback is just resistance.
Insisting on pixel-perfect at all costs.
questions.standardAnswer: In motion and dark mode system work, engineering pushback often came from implementation complexity across multiple codebases and product modules. For dark mode, the design standard could not just be visually correct; it had to be implementable across many teams on a unified timeline. I worked with engineers to turn design principles into more systematic rules and tool-supported conversion workflows. In motion work, we clarified which effects should use Lottie and which needed Baidu's internal transparent-video mechanism. The final solution was not about forcing the original design through; it was about preserving the experience goal while making implementation realistic.

questions.question: Tell me about a time you had to influence without authority.
include: Use cross-team Baidu App homepage, dark mode, or campaign infrastructure.
Mention prototypes, data, standards, leadership alignment.
Explain outcome.
avoid: Saying you persuaded people through seniority only.
questions.standardAnswer: Baidu App dark mode is a good example. The project required many business departments to adopt a unified standard and complete implementation on a shared timeline, but I did not directly manage those teams. I influenced through clear standards, research-backed rationale, tooling, and a cross-functional working group. I explained why dark mode was different from traditional night mode, defined principles around readability and visual hierarchy, and worked with engineering on implementation mechanisms. Because the path was specific and practical, teams were more willing to adopt it, and we were able to roll it out efficiently across the mobile product ecosystem.

questions.question: How do you build stakeholder alignment on a complex project?
include: Clarify goal, decision criteria, trade-offs, evidence, roles, next steps.
Use prototypes and frameworks.
Keep communication tailored to each stakeholder.
avoid: Trying to get agreement without decision criteria.
questions.standardAnswer: I build alignment by making the decision structure visible. First, I clarify the product goal, user value, business goal, constraints, and success metrics. Then I show alternatives with explicit trade-offs so stakeholders can see what we gain and what risk we accept. For complex interaction problems, I use prototypes because they make abstract ideas tangible. In Baidu App and Duxiaoxiao projects, I also tailored the conversation: leadership needed product and business logic, engineers needed feasibility details, and designers needed experience principles. Alignment becomes easier when everyone can evaluate the same decision instead of reacting to personal preferences.

questions.question: How do you communicate design rationale to non-designers?
include: Use user goals, business goals, constraints, evidence, before/after.
Avoid jargon.
Use prototypes and concrete examples.
questions.standardAnswer: I try to explain design rationale in product language. Instead of saying a design is cleaner or more modern, I explain how it improves user understanding, reduces decision cost, supports a business goal, or lowers implementation risk. I also show alternatives and explain why one direction is stronger under the current constraints. For complex ideas, especially motion, AI, or 3D interaction, I explain complex ideas through prototypes so non-designers can experience the behavior instead of imagining it from static screens.

questions.question: How do you handle conflicting stakeholder priorities?
include: Identify goals behind positions.
Create decision criteria.
Use impact, risk, effort, user value, timing.
Escalate with options when necessary.
avoid: Treating stakeholder requests as requirements automatically.
questions.standardAnswer: I first try to understand the goal behind each stakeholder's request. On large platform products, conflicts often come from legitimate but competing goals, such as engagement, revenue, user clarity, technical cost, or brand perception. Then I create a decision framework around impact, user value, business priority, effort, risk, and timing. If trade-offs are still difficult, I present clear options and consequences rather than forcing a hidden compromise. My goal is to help the team make an explicit product decision.

questions.question: How do you decide when to push back and when to compromise?
include: Push back on user trust, core task clarity, long-term quality, unsupported assumptions.
Compromise on implementation details and phased scope.
Use evidence.
questions.standardAnswer: I push back when a decision would damage core user value, trust, clarity, accessibility, or long-term product quality without a strong reason. I also push back when the assumption behind a decision is weak and we have a better way to validate it. I compromise when the core experience goal can still be preserved through a simpler implementation, phased rollout, or reuse of existing patterns. For me, the key is separating the principle from the execution detail. I try to protect the principle and stay flexible on the path.

questions.question: How do you create shared standards across multiple teams?
include: Research repeated problems, define principles, patterns, guidelines, examples, tooling, governance.
Mention dark mode and campaign infrastructure.
Include adoption.
questions.standardAnswer: I start by identifying repeated decisions that are creating inconsistency or unnecessary communication cost. Then I define principles, reusable patterns, examples, and implementation guidance. For Baidu App's dark mode, we built standards around readability, contrast, visual hierarchy, and low-light usage, but we also worked with engineering on implementation mechanisms and tool support. For homepage campaigns, we created placement rules, mutual-exclusion rules, templates, and a review process. Shared standards only work when they are clear, useful, and realistic for other teams to adopt.

questions.question: How do you make sure a design system is actually adopted?
include: Make it useful, easy to adopt, tied to real team workflows.
Include governance, education, tooling, feedback loops.
Measure adoption by usage and quality.
questions.standardAnswer: Adoption depends on whether the system solves real problems for teams. I try to connect the system to daily workflow: reusable components or patterns, clear examples, tool support, and implementation guidance. I also use design reviews and feedback loops to improve the system after teams start using it. In large organizations, governance matters, but governance alone is not enough. Teams adopt a design system when it reduces their work, improves quality, and gives them confidence that the final product will be consistent.

questions.question: Tell me about a time you failed.
include: Choose a mature, accountable story.
Duxiaoxiao's end of further investment can work if framed as product learning, not personal failure.
Explain what you learned and how it changed your judgment.
avoid: Blaming market, leadership, or technology only.
questions.standardAnswer: One difficult learning experience was Duxiaoxiao. From a design and innovation perspective, the project achieved a lot: it validated AI virtual human interaction at large scale, supported commercial revenue, and won international design awards. But after early 2023, further investment ended and the team shifted toward LLM + search. Looking back, I think one lesson is that emotional and multimodal interaction alone cannot justify a product if the core capability and device context do not create enough user value. Before mainstream LLMs, the assistant could not handle many complex questions, and mobile screens were not always the best environment for virtual-human interaction. The experience made me more rigorous about separating novelty from durable user value.

questions.question: Tell me about a time a project did not achieve the expected result.
include: Use Duxiaoxiao or an exploratory initiative.
Separate shipped quality from long-term product outcome.
Show learning about market timing, technology readiness, and value.
questions.standardAnswer: Duxiaoxiao is the clearest example. The project delivered strong design innovation, large-scale interaction data, commercial revenue, and external recognition, but it did not become a long-term continuing product after Baidu shifted focus toward LLM + search. The expected result was that a humanized AI assistant could become a durable product and brand asset. What we learned was more nuanced: some users responded strongly to companionship and emotional feedback, but for mainstream search users, efficiency, answer quality, privacy, and device context mattered more. That learning changed how I evaluate AI products. I now pay more attention to whether the AI experience improves a real user path, not only whether it feels new or expressive.

questions.question: Tell me about a time you received difficult feedback.
include: Choose feedback about communication, alignment, or being too advanced in concept.
Show specific behavior change.
Keep it credible.
avoid: Saying you cannot remember difficult feedback.
questions.standardAnswer: Earlier in my design leadership work, I received feedback that some of my exploratory concepts were strong, but the rationale was not always easy for non-design stakeholders to follow. I was moving quickly from insight to solution, while others still needed to understand the decision path. I took that seriously and changed how I presented work. I began showing the problem framing, assumptions, alternatives, trade-offs, and evidence before showing the recommended direction. That improved stakeholder alignment, especially in projects like Baidu App homepage redesigns and Duxiaoxiao, where the ideas were complex and cross-functional.

questions.question: Tell me about a time you had to change your mind.
include: Show evidence-based flexibility.
Use homepage phased approach or Duxiaoxiao limitations.
Explain what changed and why.
avoid: Pretending strong designers never change their minds.
Saying only leadership forced the change.
questions.standardAnswer: In the Baidu App homepage work, I strongly believed the product needed to move toward a more feed-led homepage. But through stakeholder discussion and experiments, I adjusted my view on speed and rollout. Instead of pushing immediately to the most aggressive version, we moved through intermediate states that preserved more of the existing search mental model. That was the right decision because the product had massive scale and strong user habits. I did not change the strategic direction, but I changed my view on how quickly the organization and users could absorb the change.

questions.question: Tell me about a time you made a decision with incomplete information.
include: Use Duxiaoxiao 0-to-1.
Explain assumptions, prototypes, staged validation, risk management.
questions.standardAnswer: Duxiaoxiao required many decisions with incomplete information because AI virtual human assistants were a new product category. We did not have a mature benchmark for how a virtual human should enter search, when it should speak, how expressive it should be, or when it should hand off to traditional search results. I made decisions by defining assumptions clearly, building prototypes, and testing the experience in real product contexts. We broke the work into interaction framework, character layer, spatial container, control layer, and production systems. The key was not to pretend we had certainty, but to make each assumption testable and keep learning from real users.

questions.question: Tell me about a time you had to earn trust from a team.
include: New ownership or cross-functional group.
Earned trust through clarity, evidence, craft, and follow-through.
Use Baidu News, Duxiaoxiao, or dark mode.
avoid: Saying trust came automatically from title.
questions.standardAnswer: When I took over Baidu News App design, the product was undergoing a strategic shift toward more professional and in-depth news. I had to earn trust from product and business stakeholders quickly because the redesign needed to change the product's tone, hierarchy, and reading experience. I started by aligning the design direction with the business strategy, then translated that into concrete changes in information hierarchy, visual tone, and reading flow. I kept the team focused on how design could make the new positioning clearer to users. The final redesign received recognition from senior leadership and won an internal innovation award, which helped build trust in the design team's judgment.

questions.question: Tell me about a time you had to lead through ambiguity.
include: Duxiaoxiao is strongest.
