Mention turning abstract vision into workstreams.
Include cross-functional coordination and outcome.
avoid: Saying ambiguity was solved by working harder.
questions.standardAnswer: Duxiaoxiao was a major ambiguity challenge. The initial idea of an AI virtual human assistant was exciting but broad: it involved AI capabilities, conversation, 3D character design, motion, search integration, brand, content production, and commercialization. My role was to turn ambiguity into direction by breaking that abstract vision into clearer workstreams: experience framework, multimodal interaction, character behavior, search container, 3D asset system, and production tools. Prototypes helped make abstract concepts tangible for product, engineering, AI, motion, and 3D teams. That structure helped the team move from an uncertain concept to a launched product that was validated by large-scale usage, business revenue, patents, and design awards.

questions.question: Tell me about a time you coached a designer through a difficult project.
include: Mention coaching around complex AI, motion, 3D, or system work.
Explain how you coached thinking, not just output.
Include result.
avoid: Taking credit away from the designer.
questions.standardAnswer: On Duxiaoxiao, I coached designers through work that was unfamiliar to many traditional UX designers, especially multimodal interaction and 3D character-related experience. My coaching focused less on giving visual feedback and more on helping them structure the problem: what user intent we were supporting, what the AI capability could actually do, what the character needed to express, and how the interface should support control and feedback. I also encouraged designers to use prototypes because static screens could not explain the interaction well. That helped the team build confidence in a new domain and produce work that could align product, engineering, and 3D teams.

questions.question: How do you raise the quality bar on a design team?
include: Standards, reviews, prototypes, coaching, implementation checks, retrospectives.
Mention turning individual expertise into team capability.
avoid: Equating quality with visual taste only.
questions.standardAnswer: I raise the quality bar by combining clear standards, strong design reviews, practical methods, and implementation follow-through. At Baidu, I taught high-fidelity prototyping as an internal instructor because I wanted to turn individual expertise into a team capability. In project work, I use design reviews to clarify rationale, compare alternatives, and identify risks early. I also care about implementation fidelity, because design quality is ultimately measured by what users experience after launch. For system work like dark mode and motion, quality also comes from reusable standards that help many teams make better decisions.

questions.question: How do you balance being hands-on with leading others?
include: Clarify you stay hands-on on critical decisions.
Delegate with frameworks and coaching.
Use Duxiaoxiao or Baidu App.
questions.standardAnswer: I stay hands-on where my involvement has the highest leverage: product architecture, interaction framework, design direction, complex trade-offs, and quality standards. At the same time, I delegate execution areas with clear context, decision criteria, and feedback loops so designers can grow. On Duxiaoxiao, I managed designers and 3D-related work, but I remained directly involved in the core experience framework and AI + search interaction. I think the right balance is to give the team ownership while still contributing where senior judgment can reduce risk or raise quality.

questions.question: How do you run design reviews?
include: Set context, decision needed, criteria, alternatives, feedback type.
Make reviews constructive and evidence-led.
Include implementation risks.
avoid: Letting senior voices dominate without criteria.
questions.standardAnswer: I run design reviews around decisions, not personal taste. I ask the presenter to clarify the problem, user goal, business goal, constraints, design alternatives, and what kind of feedback they need. Then I guide the discussion around criteria such as clarity, task efficiency, hierarchy, consistency, feasibility, and expected impact. For complex work, I like to review prototypes rather than only static screens. A good design review should help the team make a better decision, identify risks earlier, and improve the designer's thinking, not just produce a list of visual comments.

questions.question: How do you handle conflict on a team?
include: Separate people from problem.
Identify underlying goals and constraints.
Use evidence, decision criteria, and escalation if needed.
questions.standardAnswer: I try to handle conflict by first separating the people from the problem. Most conflicts in product teams come from different goals or constraints, not bad intent. I ask what each person is optimizing for, then try to create shared decision criteria around user value, business impact, feasibility, risk, and timing. If the team still disagrees, I prefer to present options with trade-offs clearly and escalate the decision when necessary. The important thing is to make the conflict productive and explicit, rather than letting it become hidden resistance.

questions.question: What is your leadership style?
include: Evidence-led, structured, hands-on where needed, coaching-oriented.
Mention raising standards and creating clarity.
Keep it human and direct.
questions.standardAnswer: My leadership style is structured, evidence-led, and hands-on where it matters. I try to create clarity in ambiguous situations, set a high quality bar, and help designers understand the reasoning behind decisions. I care about coaching because strong design teams need reusable thinking methods, not only individual feedback. At the same time, I am comfortable making hard trade-offs when the product needs a clear direction. I would describe myself as a design leader who connects strategy, craft, systems, and delivery.

questions.question: What is the hardest part of moving from China-based tech companies into the North American market?
include: Acknowledge communication and context translation.
Emphasize transferability of product complexity, scale, and methods.
Mention active adaptation through study and Vancouver context.
questions.standardAnswer: The hardest part is not the design fundamentals; many product problems are transferable. The harder part is translating context clearly. Products like Baidu App have massive scale and complex business models, but North American interviewers may not know the product, the market, or the organizational context. So I need to explain the product in familiar terms, be specific about my role, and connect the work to universal design themes such as user behavior, product strategy, experimentation, systems, and business impact. My master's program and time in Vancouver have also helped me adapt my communication style and understand the local product environment better.

questions.question: How do you adapt your communication style to a new culture or team?
include: Observe decision-making norms.
Be explicit, concise, evidence-led.
Ask clarifying questions and confirm expectations.
avoid: Treating adaptation as losing your point of view.
questions.standardAnswer: I start by observing how the team makes decisions: how direct people are, how much context they expect, what evidence they value, and how disagreement is handled. In North American teams, I try to be more explicit about my point of view, the reasoning behind it, and the trade-offs. I also ask clarifying questions earlier instead of assuming shared context. Adaptation does not mean losing my design judgment; it means making my thinking easier for the team to evaluate and use.

questions.question: What did you learn from Duxiaoxiao after further investment ended?
include: Mature business and product reflection.
Mention novelty vs durable value, technology readiness, device context, LLM shift.
Include positive retained learnings.
avoid: Saying the project was a failure.
questions.standardAnswer: I learned that a product can be innovative, well-designed, and commercially meaningful, but still not become the long-term direction if technology, market timing, and user value do not align. Duxiaoxiao achieved a lot: large-scale interaction, monetization revenue, patents, and international awards. But before mainstream LLMs, the assistant's ability to answer complex questions was limited, and on mobile, virtual-human interaction was not always more efficient than traditional search. When further investment ended and the team shifted toward LLM + search, it made me more careful about evaluating AI products. I now ask whether the AI improves a real user path, whether users understand its boundaries, and whether the experience can create durable value beyond novelty.

questions.question: What does good design leadership look like to you?
include: Clarity, quality, systems, talent growth, business/user balance, shipped impact.
Mention design as strategic function.
avoid: Making leadership only about managing people.
questions.standardAnswer: Good design leadership means helping teams make better product decisions and ship better user experiences. It includes setting a clear direction, raising the quality bar, creating reusable systems, coaching designers, and building alignment with product, engineering, data, and business teams. I also think good design leaders connect design to business impact without losing user value. Design leadership is not only about managing designers; it is about making design a strategic function that improves the product, the team, and the organization over time.

questions.question: Why did Duxiaoxiao not continue as a long-term product?
include: Frame it as a business and product judgment, not bitterness.
Mention Baidu's focus on utility-driven AI and search efficiency.
Explain pre-LLM conversation limitations and mobile form-factor constraints.
Preserve the positive learning and product value.
avoid: Blaming leadership, market, or technology only.
Calling the whole project a failure.
Ignoring the product's large-scale validation and commercial value.
questions.standardAnswer:
Duxiaoxiao achieved meaningful results, but after early 2023, further investment ended and the team shifted toward LLM + search. I think that was a reasonable business and product decision.

There were three main reasons. First, Baidu's stronger strategic focus was utility-driven AI and search efficiency, while Duxiaoxiao's strongest user value was often emotional expression and companionship. Second, before mainstream LLMs, conversational AI still had clear limitations. It could handle some knowledge and intent-based scenarios, but it was not strong enough for complex, open-ended conversations. Third, mobile was not always the best form factor for virtual-human interaction. A face-to-face-style experience on a small screen could feel expressive, but not always efficient.

The project was valuable because it gave us real production learning about AI trust, emotional response, user expectations, and multimodal interaction. But durable AI products need strong core capability, not only a compelling interface layer.

questions.question: Why should we hire you?
include: Clear value proposition.
Mention complex product design, AI, systems, business impact, leadership, technical fluency.
Connect to company needs.
avoid: Saying only that you work hard.
questions.standardAnswer: I think I would be a strong fit if you need a product designer who can handle complex product problems at both strategic and execution levels. I bring 12 years of experience across large-scale consumer platforms, AI product design, design systems, and cross-functional leadership. I have designed for products with hundreds of millions of users, led 0-to-1 AI assistant experiences, built platform-level design infrastructure, and connected design decisions to measurable business outcomes. I also bring technical fluency, so I can collaborate closely with engineers and prototype complex interactions when needed. I can contribute not only to interface quality, but also to product direction, team alignment, and long-term experience systems.

questions.question: What makes you different from other senior designers?
include: Differentiate with combination, not superiority.
Mention scale, AI, systems, business, technical fluency, leadership.
questions.standardAnswer: What makes me different is the combination of product scale, AI interaction experience, systems thinking, and technical fluency. Many designers are strong in one or two of those areas. My background connects them. I have worked on Baidu App at massive scale, led Duxiaoxiao as a 0-to-1 AI virtual human assistant, built design infrastructure used across teams, and managed designers while staying hands-on. I bring a systems lens to product design and can move from strategy to execution across UX architecture, UI craft, prototyping, metrics, and cross-functional leadership. That range is where I can create the most value.

questions.question: What kind of impact do you want to have here?
include: Tie impact to product, team, and design function.
Mention learning first, then contribution.
Avoid assuming too much about company.
avoid: Saying you want to change everything.
questions.standardAnswer: I would want to have impact in three areas. First, I want to help the product solve meaningful user problems with clear, high-quality experiences. Second, I want to contribute to product strategy by bringing design judgment into problem framing, prioritization, and trade-offs. Third, I want to help the team build reusable systems and standards that improve quality and delivery over time. I would start by learning the product, users, business model, and team workflow, then look for places where my experience in platform UX, AI, systems, and cross-functional execution can create leverage.

questions.question: How do you think AI will change product design?
include: Discuss design process and product experience.
Mention AI as capability inside workflows, not just tools.
Include trust, boundaries, evaluation, human role.
questions.standardAnswer: AI will change product design in two ways. First, it will change the design workflow by helping designers explore, prototype, generate content, and test ideas faster. Second, and more importantly, it will change product experiences themselves. More products will include AI agents, generated answers, personalized interfaces, and multimodal interaction. That means designers need to think about trust, user control, explainability, failure states, privacy, and long-term human-AI relationships. My view is that AI does not reduce the need for product design judgment; it increases it, because the experience becomes more dynamic and less predictable.

questions.question: What is your point of view on AI assistants and human-AI interaction?
include: Use Duxiaoxiao learnings.
Balance usefulness, efficiency, emotional response, trust, dependency, boundaries.
Mention multimodal and LLM context.
avoid: Treating AI assistants as only chat UI.
questions.standardAnswer: My point of view is that AI assistants are not only a question-answering interface. They create a relationship between the user and the system, and that relationship needs to be designed carefully. From Duxiaoxiao, I learned that some users value emotional response, companionship, and expression, while mainstream users still care deeply about efficiency, answer quality, control, and privacy. Multimodal interaction can make AI feel more understandable and approachable, but it can also create higher expectations and dependency risks. Good AI assistant design should make the AI useful, transparent, controllable, and emotionally appropriate for the context.

questions.question: How do you think about design's role in business impact?
include: Design shapes user behavior, trust, conversion, retention, efficiency.
Use homepage revenue estimate and monetization guardrails.
Avoid reducing design to growth hacking.
avoid: Saying design impact is only qualitative.
Saying business impact is only revenue.
questions.standardAnswer: I think design creates business impact by shaping user understanding, behavior, trust, efficiency, and long-term engagement. In Baidu App homepage redesigns, design decisions around hierarchy and content discovery affected DAU, time spent, search/feed engagement, and estimated revenue. But business impact should not mean blindly pushing metrics. In homepage monetization work, I also pushed for rules to protect the core user experience. Good design connects user value and business value so the product can grow without damaging trust or clarity.

questions.question: What concerns do you think we might have about your background?
include: Address China-based experience, recent education/career gap, English communication, level fit.
Turn concerns into strengths with evidence.
Be direct and calm.
questions.standardAnswer: You might have questions about how my China-based experience transfers to a North American product team, or whether my recent computer science master's degree means I am moving away from design. My answer is that the product context is different, but the core problems are very transferable: user behavior, product strategy, experimentation, design systems, AI interaction, business trade-offs, and cross-functional alignment. The master's degree was not a career pivot away from design; it strengthened my technical fluency as a designer. I am also actively adapting how I communicate my work so the context is clear for North American teams.

questions.question: How should we interpret your China-based experience in a North American product context?
include: Translate scale and product types.
Emphasize transferable complexity.
Acknowledge local-market learning.
Mention Baidu App analogy.
questions.standardAnswer: I would interpret it as high-scale product design experience in a different but very demanding market. Baidu App, for example, can be understood as a China-specific super app-style product combining search, content discovery, short video, AI assistants, and service entry points. The exact market is different from North America, but the design challenges are highly transferable: how to support different user intents, how to evolve a product architecture, how to validate changes at scale, how to balance business and user needs, and how to align many teams. I also know I need to keep learning the local context, but I believe the complexity and depth of my past work are directly relevant.

questions.question: If you redesigned Duxiaoxiao in the LLM era, what would you do differently?
include: Start from utility and user workflows before the avatar layer.
Mention stronger LLM capabilities for complex questions and task support.
Include trust, sources, boundaries, confidence, and user control.
Explain selective use of the virtual human layer.
avoid: Saying the answer is simply to add an LLM.
Making the virtual human layer the whole product strategy.
Ignoring trust, privacy, and generated-answer risks.
questions.standardAnswer:
I would start from utility and user workflows first, then decide where the virtual human layer adds value. With LLMs, the assistant could now handle more complex questions, multi-turn reasoning, summarization, and task support, so the core capability would be much stronger than it was before.

I would make three changes. First, I would integrate the assistant more deeply into real search and task flows instead of treating it mainly as a separate character experience. Second, I would make the AI's boundaries, sources, confidence, and controls much clearer, because trust becomes more important when answers are generated. Third, I would use the avatar selectively. It should appear when emotional tone, guidance, or explanation helps the user, but stay out of the way when speed and information density matter more.

In short, I would not make it a virtual human with AI. I would make it a useful AI assistant that uses a virtual human layer only where that layer improves understanding, trust, or emotional fit.

questions.question: What are your compensation expectations?
include: Keep flexible and market-aligned.
Reference level, scope, location, and total compensation.
Ask for the company's range if appropriate.
questions.standardAnswer: I am looking for a market-aligned package based on the role's level, scope, location, and total compensation structure. At this stage, I would prefer to understand how your team is leveling the role and what range you have budgeted. If we are aligned on scope and level, I am confident we can have a practical compensation discussion.

questions.question: Are you considering other offers?
include: Be truthful.
Keep it concise.
Signal active search without pressure.
questions.standardAnswer: I am actively interviewing and speaking with teams, but I would prefer to keep the details confidential. What I can say is that I am prioritizing role fit, team fit, product complexity, and the opportunity to create meaningful design impact.

questions.question: What would you need to feel comfortable accepting an offer?
include: Mention role scope, level, manager/team fit, product direction, compensation, work model, growth.
Keep criteria mature and practical.
questions.standardAnswer: I would want clarity on the role scope, level expectations, team structure, product priorities, manager relationship, and how design contributes to product decisions. Compensation and work model also matter, but the most important factor is whether the role can use my strengths in product design, AI or platform complexity, systems thinking, and cross-functional leadership. If those are aligned, I would feel comfortable making a decision quickly.

questions.question: What level are you expecting?
include: State Senior/Staff/Product Design Lead fit.
Explain based on scope, not title entitlement.
Stay open to company leveling.
avoid: Being rigid before understanding leveling system.
Comparing titles across companies too literally.
questions.standardAnswer: Based on my experience, I would expect to be considered around Senior Product Designer, Staff Product Designer, or Product Design Lead level, depending on how your company defines scope and leveling. My background includes Staff-level product design, platform architecture, AI product leadership, design systems, business impact, and people leadership. I am open to discussing the right level based on the actual expectations of the role.

questions.question: Do you need any immigration support now or in the future?
include: State current Canada work authorization.
Clarify no sponsorship needed in Canada.
For non-Canada roles, support depends on local requirements.
questions.standardAnswer: For Canada, I do not need employer sponsorship. My three-year Canadian post-graduation work permit is currently being processed, and I currently have legal authorization to work for Canadian employers. For opportunities outside Canada, work authorization would depend on the local requirements of that country or region.

questions.question: What is your earliest start date?
include: State immediate availability.
Mention reasonable coordination if needed.
questions.standardAnswer: I am available to start immediately. If there are onboarding steps or scheduling needs on your side, I can coordinate around those.
